video_title: “Don’t Bet Against Elon” | Why SpaceX Could Change Everything
gist: Ahead of SpaceX's $1.75T IPO (priced at $135 by Elon Musk himself with only 5% free float and ~30% allocated to retail), Elizabeth Thomas of SoFi argues the deal will set governance/pricing precedents for OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs while warning of high odds it breaks IPO price early; she expects new Fed chair Kevin Warsh to read dovish and trigger a bull steepener. Apex CEO Bill Capuzzi details the retail-driven boom in 0DTE options, prediction markets and perpetual futures, the under-discussed DTCC settlement/capital risk around the IPO, and a rotation in May out of semis/energy into AI infrastructure and memory chips — while flagging a frothy, correction-prone market.

insights.claim: SpaceX is going public at a $1.75 trillion valuation with only 5% of shares free-floating, a structure that constrains supply and could push the price higher.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: A mega-cap IPO with a tiny float means scarcity-driven price dynamics; thin supply can amplify both upside spikes and downside air pockets, so size positions for volatility.
insights.quote: this company is going public at $1.75 trillion. Going public at a mega cap valuation, which is wild.

insights.claim: Elon Musk set the SpaceX IPO price himself at $135 rather than going through the usual banker price-discovery process, an unusual move that removes the typical pricing back-and-forth.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Issuer-set pricing bypasses bookbuilding and could reshape banker fee structures and IPO mechanics for future mega deals to watch.
insights.quote: Billy Ray Valentine has set the price and in this case that would be Elon Musk and he's basically told people okay it's $135

insights.claim: SpaceX is making roughly 30% of its IPO shares available to retail, with retail already bidding about $70 billion against only about $75 billion of shares available.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Massive retail demand into a tiny float signals strong day-one buying pressure but also fragile holders; the oversubscription sets a retail-allocation precedent for future IPOs.
insights.quote: retail had already bid about $70 billion... Apparently, there's only $75 billion worth of shares being available.

insights.claim: SpaceX is being fast-tracked into the Nasdaq-100 (NDX) in roughly 15 days due to its size, which will shift index weightings and force passive flows.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Rapid index inclusion forces index funds to buy, adding mechanical demand but also concentrating index exposure to a volatile new name.
insights.quote: they're fasttracking into sort of the NDX and the NDX 100. I think it's going to be 15 days.

insights.claim: A successful SpaceX IPO sets the precedent and stage for OpenAI and Anthropic to go public later in the year, though both have only filed confidentially and could still pull their offerings.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: SpaceX is a read-through proxy for the AI IPO pipeline; a weak debut could chill or delay the OpenAI/Anthropic listings.
insights.quote: this sets the precedent and sets the stage for the possibility of anthropic and open AAI going public later this year

insights.claim: The chances that SpaceX breaks its $135 IPO price within the first one to two months of trading are high because first-year IPO drawdowns are large and the company is newly transparent and not yet profitable.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Expect the stock to trade below $135 at some point early; longer-term believers should have tight sell discipline and a decade-plus horizon rather than chasing the debut.
insights.quote: the chances of it at some point breaking its IPO price, I think, are probably pretty high. I think the first month, two months of this trading is going to be all over the place.

insights.claim: SpaceX should be treated as a decades-long thesis (space exploration, life on Mars, data centers in space) rather than a one-year trade, so investors buying during the volatile first 12 months need a tight thesis and sell discipline.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Frames position sizing and time horizon: only multi-decade conviction holders should weather first-year volatility; traders should expect whipsaws.
insights.quote: these are decadesl long ideas... make sure that you've got your thesis tight, make sure you've got your sell discipline tight

insights.claim: SpaceX's atypical capital and governance structure differs sharply from normal public companies and will likely become a template that influences future IPO structures and public-company governance.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Watch for copycat governance structures (concentrated control, low float) in subsequent high-profile IPOs as a structural shift in public markets.
insights.quote: the capital structure, the governance structure of this company are so different from anything that we normally see in public companies

insights.claim: New Fed chair Kevin Warsh is likely to be interpreted as more dovish than the market has priced at his first press conference, causing the market's expected 2026 rate hike to be partially priced out and the 2-year yield to rally.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Thomas used the 2-year Treasury as a final trade on this view; a dovish read could rally front-end bonds, so long the 2-year ahead of the presser.
insights.quote: there is a decent chance that he gets interpreted as a little bit more dovish than what the market has priced in. And we see that hike start to get priced out.

insights.claim: Warsh inherits a deeply divided Fed plus expectations of both one rate hike this year and dovishness, making consensus-building unusually hard for an incoming chair.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Policy uncertainty and conflicting mandates raise the odds of communication missteps and rate volatility during the leadership transition.
insights.quote: We've got a very very divided Fed right now. So building consensus is going to be extremely difficult.

insights.claim: A bull steepener — the 2-year yield falling while the 10-year yield rises — has a high probability in Warsh's first months, but the Fed would respond by shifting balance-sheet duration rather than using interest rates to control the curve.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Curve steepening is typically read as positive for banks; watch the Fed's balance-sheet duration mix (rolling off the short end, buying the long end) as the real lever, not the funds rate.
insights.quote: This would be what's called a bull steepener where the 2-year yield falls and the 10-year yield rises. I do think there is a high probability of that happening.

insights.claim: Warsh wants the Fed's balance sheet smaller and could control the long end of the curve by letting short-end exposure roll off and reinvesting modestly into longer duration.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thomas
insights.so_what: Signals a shift toward balance-sheet-driven yield-curve management; bond investors should watch QT composition rather than just rate guidance.
insights.quote: he could effectively very slowly reduce the Fed's balance sheet by just kind of shifting around the duration and he could control the long end of the curve if he wanted to.

themes.theme: Retail product proliferation: 0DTE, prediction markets, perpetuals

insights.claim: Apex Fintech Solutions operates as the 'AWS of investing,' providing back-end clearing and infrastructure for about 250 B2B clients (including SoFi, Stash, Webull, State Street, Coinbase) supporting roughly 40 million retail investors.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Apex's aggregate data across 40M accounts is a high-signal proxy for broad retail positioning and flows; its infrastructure is a single point of dependence for major fintechs.
insights.quote: there's roughly 250 clients. We support about 40 million retail investors at this point.

insights.claim: Prediction markets are being adopted by retail not just for sports but to hedge equity positions and express macro views, with adoption demand 'off the charts.'
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Prediction markets are evolving into a hedging/macro-expression asset class; example given was using device-sales predictions to hedge an Apple position.
insights.quote: people are using these things to properly hedge or express an investment thesis on a macro basis

insights.claim: The adoption curve for prediction markets, perpetual futures and similar products will compress into roughly the next 5 years, versus the ~50 years it took listed options (launched 1973) to reach scale.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: A compressed adoption curve means these products could rapidly grow large enough to affect market structure; position for faster mainstreaming than options had.
insights.quote: It took better part of 50s something years to get to where we are. I think we're going to see that that same adoption curve condense over the course of the next 5 years

insights.claim: Perpetual futures are largely traded offshore, unregulated, and require US citizens to access them illegally via different IP addresses, posing real risk versus regulated CME products that trade 24/7.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Favors regulated venues (CME micro Bitcoin futures, 24/7) over offshore perpetuals; regulatory legitimacy is a differentiator as these products mainstream.
insights.quote: they're being traded largely offshore. They're not particularly regulated... you actually have to trade them illegally, right?

insights.claim: Combo/parlay bets on prediction markets are almost all losers, returning roughly 45% worse than parlays on sports bets, signaling euphoria and a lack of investor education around these products.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: A red flag on retail speculation in prediction combos; reinforces that the products are being used for gambling-like risk without education or safeguards.
insights.quote: the returns on the combos on predictions were far worse uh like 45% % worse return than a parlay on a bet, sports bet.

insights.claim: Tokenized securities (e.g., a tokenized version of Nvidia available overseas but not in the US) are not yet legal domestically because the SEC is still doing diligence, and Apex refuses to offer them despite having ~30% of clients outside the US.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Tokenized US equities remain a regulatory gray zone; the fact they exist offshore but not onshore is itself a risk signal for investors tempted by them.
insights.quote: there is out there you can go buy a tokenized version of Nvidia, but you can't buy it here in the states. That in of itself should tell you something

themes.theme: Market structure, settlement risk and infrastructure

insights.claim: Retail now makes up roughly double the share of the market it did 20 years ago, and about 50% of Americans have an investment account versus single digits in markets like Brazil and India, leaving large international growth runway.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Rising retail share increases the market's sensitivity to retail-driven products and sentiment; emerging-market account penetration is a long-term growth vector for brokerage infrastructure.
insights.quote: we have roughly 50% of the people in the United States have some investment account when you get into places like Brazil or India the the number is single digits

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO will create significant under-the-radar dislocation in DTCC settlement, capital usage and risk between Friday and Tuesday, an infrastructure stress point that gets little airtime.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Watch for back-end settlement/capital strain around the IPO; Capuzzi sits on DTCC's board, lending weight to the warning of behind-the-scenes risk.
insights.quote: there's going to be a lot of dislocation behind the scenes in terms of capital usage and risk.

insights.claim: Most industry back-end infrastructure (including DTCC and big banks) still runs on batch-processed IBM mainframe COBOL code, which is incompatible with the 24/7 real-time data needed for AI co-pilots and continuous trading.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Legacy batch infrastructure is the bottleneck preventing weekend/24-7 equity trading and AI-native finance; firms with real-time platforms (like Apex Ascend OS) hold a structural edge.
insights.quote: The back end of our industry is stuck in this spot... we go through this ridiculous thing called a batch

insights.claim: Within roughly the next 12 months retail will likely be able to buy and sell equities like Nvidia on Saturdays and Sundays as real-time infrastructure replaces legacy batch systems.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Weekend equity trading would extend market hours toward crypto-like 24/7, reshaping liquidity patterns and brokerage operations; a concrete near-term catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: my bet is in the next 12 months you'll see people buying and selling, you know, equities Saturday, Sunday.

insights.claim: Apex rebuilt its platform (Ascend OS, started ~2021) as a real-time, 24-hour licensed SaaS model with no batch concept, positioning it to train AI co-pilots on real-time data and eventually replace 1%-fee human advisors with always-on financial co-pilots.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Real-time data platforms are the prerequisite for AI financial co-pilots; this is a competitive moat and a threat to traditional advice fees.
insights.quote: today what we do is we hand somebody a check and we pay him 1% to do that. Um you're going to have a machine next to you

themes.theme: Retail flows and rotation signals (Apex May Investor Pulse)

insights.claim: In May, retail investors rotated heavily into memory chips — Micron was the number-one net-bought name, with SanDisk and DRAM-related names in the top 10 net purchases.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Retail conviction concentrated in memory as an AI-adjacent play; a leading indicator of where self-directed flows are crowding.
insights.quote: Micron was the number one net bought name for the month SanDisk DRAM were in the top 10 net purchases

insights.claim: Retail bought the 'broadening' AI infrastructure trade in May — CoreWeave, ARM, Qualcomm, Cisco and Marvell ranked in the top 20 net buys — favoring networking, cooling and plumbing names over headline Nvidia.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Signals a rotation from the obvious AI leader into the architecture/networking/cooling layer; watch these names as the next leg of the AI trade.
insights.quote: this is about the architecture the networking the cooling systems the apex of sorts the plumbing parts of AI is what we saw people rotating in

insights.claim: Retail net-sold semiconductors (Broadcom, Taiwan Semi, ASML) and energy (Chevron, Exxon, Freeport) in May, with the energy selling tied to easing Middle East tensions rather than a wholesale exit to cash.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: The move was a rotation, not de-risking — capital flowed from semis/energy into AI infrastructure and memory; geopolitical de-escalation drove the energy sell.
insights.quote: big selloff in energy in May, Chevron, Exxon, Freeport where tension started to die off as to what was going on in the Middle East.

insights.claim: About 70% of Apex's 40 million investors are Gen Z or Millennial (with even-younger Gen Alpha emerging), skewing the retail flow data toward a younger cohort.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: The young skew means Apex's flow data reflects younger-investor behavior and risk appetite, useful for reading newer-product adoption like prediction markets and 0DTE.
insights.quote: 70% of that 40 million is either Gen Z or Millennial

insights.claim: A large pool of retail cash is sitting on the sidelines waiting, and much of it is expected to flow into the SpaceX IPO.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Sidelined cash earmarked for SpaceX supports day-one demand but could leave other parts of the market starved of inflows around the IPO window.
insights.quote: there's a lot of money sitting on the table waiting. We expect a lot of that's going to go into in and around uh this IPO

insights.claim: A wave of insiders and companies (Google's large offering, SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta's coming capital needs, Oracle and the neoclouds raising capital, plus tech insider selling) are selling stock to top-tick a frothy market, raising concerns about whether retail can absorb the supply.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Heavy equity supply from savvy insiders near peaks is a classic distribution signal; when sellers outnumber buyers the market tends to turn, so monitor IPO/secondary supply absorption.
insights.quote: all of a sudden you see lots of really savvy you know what I mean like companies or whatever starting to sell their stock

insights.claim: IPO issuers deliberately try to time the market top, so the current cluster of mega raises (SpaceX, Anthropic, Cerberus) reflects insiders judging the market frothy and trying to sell at the peak.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Use IPO clustering as a top-timing signal; a surge of insider-led offerings historically precedes turns when seller volume overwhelms buyers.
insights.quote: what these all are do, these guys are doing is they're looking at the top and saying, okay, frothy market... I can toptick this thing

insights.claim: The market feels too frothy and a correction is likely sooner than later, with bad inflation prints and scary news being shrugged off only because the AI trade has kept the market rolling through luck.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Sets up a cautious tactical stance; the AI trade is the single pillar holding the market up, so any crack in AI sentiment plus sticky inflation could trigger the expected correction.
insights.quote: it feels like, you know, we're in this position where there's going to be some correction sooner than later, right? It just doesn't it feels too frothy

insights.claim: The AI trade was the key factor that kept the market 'on the tracks' from late last year into this year despite a backdrop of bad macro news.
insights.speaker: Bill Capuzzi
insights.so_what: Frames AI as the load-bearing driver of the entire rally; its durability is the central risk to monitor for the broader market.
insights.quote: we had this AI trade to keep this train on the tracks and rolling.
